company.name: Gamma
company.tagline: From a 'modern documents' tool nobody bought to a $100M ARR profitable business with 50 people
company.summary: Gamma is the rare AI hit that wasn't a 2023 ChatGPT wrapper. The team spent two and a half years quietly building a 'modern documents' editor that didn't take off — then bolted GPT onto it in March 2023 and grew from 60K to 3M users in three months. The company stayed lean, stayed profitable, and reached $100M ARR with around 50 employees and only $23M raised before the round that made them a unicorn.

events.title: Gamma founded
events.description: Three ex-Optimizely colleagues — Grant Lee (CEO, ex-CFO at Optimizely), James Fox (CTO), Jon Noronha (CPO) — incorporate Gamma. The thesis: PowerPoint is broken, knowledge work needs a 'modern document' that blends deck, doc, and webpage.

events.title: Private beta opens
events.description: After ten months of nights-and-weekends prototyping, Gamma opens a private beta of its non-AI 'modern documents' editor — a card-based tool emphasizing design and embeddable content.

events.title: Seed $7M (Accel)
events.description: Led by Accel. Angels include Zoom CEO Eric Yuan, ex-LinkedIn CEO Jeff Weiner, and founders of Airtable, Patreon, Segment, Honey, and Optimizely. South Park Commons, LocalGlobe, Afore, Hustle Fund participate.

events.title: Public launch — pre-AI
events.description: Gamma exits beta as a non-AI 'card-based modern document' editor. Reaches roughly 60K users by early 2023 — moderate traction, not a hit. By Lee's later Lenny's Podcast retelling, an early investor called the product 'the dumbest idea I've heard.'

events.title: AI rebuild ships
events.description: After a three-month internal sprint, Gamma re-launches as 'a new medium for presenting ideas, powered by GPT-4.' Type a prompt, get a full deck. Servers crash for three days under load.

events.title: Product Hunt relaunch
events.description: Gamma re-launches on Product Hunt as the AI version. Combined with an organic Twitter moment, signups jump from 2,000/day to 60,000/day. By June, Gamma is at roughly 3M users.

events.title: First paying customers
events.description: Within two months of turning on payments, Gamma crosses $1M ARR. Users had been throwing money over the wall during the server crashes assuming payment was the fix.

events.title: Seed extension (angels)
events.description: A small, quiet seed top-up from angel investors after the AI rebuild's traction is undeniable. Total raised to date sits around $11M. The team explicitly chooses not to chase a large round.

events.title: 10M users
events.description: Nine months after the AI rebuild, Gamma's user count crosses ten million. Headcount: under 20. Revenue per employee already dwarfing typical SaaS benchmarks.

events.title: Profitability
events.description: Gamma turns profitable. The team will later cite 15+ consecutive months of profitability — running with more cash in the bank than they had ever raised.

events.title: Series A $12M (Accel)
events.description: Accel doubles down. Tiny round by 2024 AI standards — the team frames it as optional, not necessary. Total funding to date: ~$23M.

events.title: Tome layoffs hit press
events.description: Direct competitor Tome — which had raised $80M+ — restructures and lays off ~20% of staff in April, then a further ~31% later in the year. The 'well-funded competitor collapses' contrast becomes part of Gamma's investor narrative.

events.title: $30M ARR · 50M users
events.description: Year-end checkpoint. Sacra estimates ~$30.5M ARR. User base around 50 million. Headcount ~30. The 'small team, large product' narrative becomes a recurring press hook.

events.title: Gamma 2.0
events.description: Gamma 2.0 ships: presentations, websites, and social posts from a single prompt. The product positioning explicitly widens from 'AI presentations' to 'AI design partner.'

events.title: $50M ARR (35-person team)
events.description: Grant Lee discloses $50M ARR with roughly 35 employees. Revenue per employee crosses $1.4M — the headline becomes the small-team story.

events.title: Gamma 3.0 + AI Agent
events.description: Gamma 3.0 ships globally with an AI design agent — natural-language editing, web research, restyling across an entire document. The platform shifts from 'generator' to 'design partner.'

events.title: Series B $68M / $2.1B
events.description: Led by a16z, with Accel and Uncork. Includes secondary for early employees. Disclosed simultaneously: $100M ARR, 70M users. Founder Grant Lee posts a long-form 'how we built a $100M business differently' essay the same day.

events.title: Lenny's Podcast
events.description: Grant Lee on Lenny Rachitsky: '"Dumbest idea I've heard" to $100M ARR.' Episode covers PG's early dismissal, the onboarding rebuild, the 1,000+ creator program, and the deliberate small-team strategy.

events.title: Gamma Imagine
events.description: Gamma Imagine launches — a standalone AI design surface for logos, infographics, diagrams, and social posts. The platform-tier expansion from documents into general visual design assets.

platforms.name: X (Twitter)
platforms.description: Grant Lee writes long-form retrospective threads on X — the early-investor 'dumbest idea' story, the 95% drop-off teardown, the small-team revenue-per-employee comparisons. These threads become the source material every secondary creator quotes from.
platforms.catalyst: March 2023 Product Hunt + Twitter relaunch as 'a new medium for presenting ideas, powered by GPT-4.' Signups went from 2,000/day to 60,000/day. Servers crashed for three days.
platforms.whenItWorks: When the founder personally has the appetite to write retrospectives that reveal real numbers — drop-off rates, headcount, revenue. Specificity drives the share rate
platforms.whenItDoesnt: If the founder posts only product news. Retrospectives with concrete numbers and humility ('PG called it dumb') outperform polished launch tweets every time

platforms.name: YouTube
platforms.description: YouTube is where Gamma's distribution actually compounds. Two surfaces: long-form podcast appearances (Lenny, Sequoia's Training Data, a16z), and a 150+ creator program producing daily product walkthroughs in the productivity-tool genre. The combination owns the search query 'AI presentation tool review.'
platforms.catalyst: Grant Lee on Lenny's Podcast (Nov 13, 2025): '"Dumbest idea I've heard" to $100M ARR.' Bundled with the Series B announcement three days earlier — funding press + founder long-form video into the same week.
platforms.whenItWorks: When the product is highly visual and the result is screen-recordable in under 60 seconds. Creator economics work because the demo IS the content — no separate production needed
platforms.whenItDoesnt: Don't lean on YouTube creators if the 'aha' isn't visible in a single screen recording. If the value requires a setup, the format won't carry it

platforms.name: LinkedIn
platforms.description: LinkedIn is the natural home for Gamma's actual buyer — the consultant, founder, salesperson, educator who lives in slides. Grant Lee's milestone announcements ($50M ARR with 35 people, the $100M ARR essay) consistently outperform on LinkedIn vs X for absolute reach.
platforms.catalyst: Grant Lee's LinkedIn post announcing the $12M Series A (May 2024): explicit framing that the round was small by design, paired with the headcount and ARR numbers. Reposted hundreds of times by founders citing it as the counter-example to the AI mega-round trend.
platforms.whenItWorks: When the audience is non-developer knowledge workers and the content frames the company as a counter-narrative ('we did it with a small team / less capital'). LinkedIn rewards that frame
platforms.whenItDoesnt: Pure product-update posts. LinkedIn punishes anything that reads like a press release

platforms.name: Reddit
platforms.description: Reddit is where the queries 'Gamma vs Tome,' 'Gamma vs Beautiful.AI,' 'is Gamma worth paying for' actually get answered. Threads in r/ChatGPT, r/productivity, r/Notion, and r/EntrepreneurRideAlong have done more for Gamma's bottom-funnel conversion than any paid acquisition.
platforms.catalyst: No single moment. The slow 2023–2025 emergence of Reddit threads naming Gamma as the default 'AI presentation' answer — the kind of recurring social proof you can't buy.
platforms.whenItWorks: When the product has clear free-tier value that users can validate in one session. Reddit wisdom-of-crowd recommendations only happen for products people actually use
platforms.whenItDoesnt: Don't seed threads. The community detects astroturfing within hours and the backlash is permanent

platforms.name: Hacker News
platforms.description: Less load-bearing for Gamma than for developer-tool companies, but the front-page placement of the March 2023 AI relaunch and Gamma 3.0 functioned as 'serious technical attention' proof — visible to investors, employees, and competitors at the same time.
platforms.catalyst: March 2023 AI relaunch front-page thread. Combined with the Product Hunt #1 placement, it produced the technical-credibility ceiling that supported the ARR ramp.
platforms.whenItWorks: When the launch has a clear technical novelty — multimodel orchestration, an agent layer, a non-trivial inference pipeline. HN rewards substance
platforms.whenItDoesnt: For consumer-facing UX iterations, HN is the wrong room. Don't expect it to carry incremental design updates

platforms.name: Instagram
platforms.description: Higher score than for a B2B dev tool because Gamma's output is genuinely visual and the audience overlaps with Instagram's design-curious knowledge workers. But it's still a secondary channel — creators redistribute YouTube clips here, the company itself doesn't lead with IG.
platforms.catalyst: Reels showing the prompt-to-finished-deck flow in 30 seconds. The visual delta between blank canvas and polished output is exactly what Instagram's algorithm rewards.
platforms.whenItWorks: When the product output is screen-recordable, visually striking, and the 'before / after' delta is large. Gamma qualifies; most B2B SaaS doesn't
platforms.whenItDoesnt: Don't spin up an IG presence as the primary channel. As a secondary clip-redistribution surface for YouTube wins, fine. As a lead source, near zero ROI
